docs.title: MaskMe: Data Privacy & Anonymization Framework
docs.text:
Welcome to MaskMe, a professional framework designed for secure data anonymization and privacy risk analysis.

MaskMe allows Data Engineers and Researchers to transform sensitive datasets into privacy-compliant versions while maintaining data utility for statistical analysis.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Plug-and-play Strategies: 6 built-in anonymization methods.
  • Agnostic I/O: Support for CSV, JSON, and JSONL.
  • Extensible Registry: Easily add your own custom strategies.
  • CI/CD Ready: Integrated with GitHub Actions for automated quality assurance.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Anonymize a CSV file using a config file
maskme mask input.csv --config rules.yaml --output clean_data.csv

docs.title: Architecture: The Anonymization Engine
docs.text: The MaskMe engine is built on a Registry Pattern, ensuring a strict separation between the orchestration logic and the transformation rules.

docs.title: The Transformation Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Registry Lookup: The engine identifies the requested strategy alias (e.g., hash) from the configuration.
  2. Configuration Injection: Parameters (like salt) are dynamically injected into the strategy instance.
  3. Streaming Pipeline: Data is processed record-by-record to ensure a low memory footprint, even with large datasets.

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
• Extensibility: New strategies can be registered without modifying the core engine.
  • Immutability: The engine never modifies the source data; it always produces a new stream or file.
  • Type Safety: Leveraging Python type hints and Pydantic (upcoming) for configuration validation.

docs.title: Anonymization Strategies
docs.text: MaskMe provides six primary strategies to address different privacy requirements.

docs.title: 1. Hash Strategy (hash)
docs.text:
• Logic: Irreversible pseudonymization using cryptographic hashing.
  • Formula: $\(y = H(x + \text{salt})\)$
  • Best for: Unique identifiers (IDs, Usernames).

docs.title: 2. Noise Strategy (noise)
docs.text:
• Logic: Adds a random value within a specified range to numerical data.
  • Formula: $\(y = x + \epsilon, \text{where } \epsilon \in [-\text{range}, +\text{range}]\)$
  • Best for: Salaries, ages, or sensor metrics.

docs.title: 3. Redaction Strategy (redact)
docs.text:
• Logic: Replaces the entire value with a static placeholder.
  • Example: John Doe \(\rightarrow\) [REDACTED]
  • Best for: Names, emails, or physical addresses.

docs.title: 4. Generalization Strategy (generalize)
docs.text:
• Logic: Reduces the precision of data by mapping values to intervals.
  • Example: Age: 23 \(\rightarrow\) Age: 20-30
  • Best for: Birth years or geographic locations to increase k-anonymity.

docs.title: 5. Masking Strategy (mask)
docs.text:
• Logic: Hides parts of a string while keeping some characters visible for context.
  • Example: 4500-1234-5678 \(\rightarrow\) ****-****-5678
  • Best for: Credit card numbers or phone numbers.

docs.title: 6. Drop Strategy (drop)
docs.text:
• Logic: Entirely removes the column from the output dataset.
  • Best for: High-risk identifiers that are not needed for analysis (e.g., SSN).
